guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG): Rheumatology — Psoriatic arthritis and spondyloarthritis (current)
ASAS-EULAR recommendations for the management of axial spondyloarthritis 2022
NICE NG59: Low back pain and sciatica in over 16s — cauda equina red flags
intentional_test_features: OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE test: an alarming acute presentation (severe axial pain, inability to weight-bear, temporary bedbound state, 'spine feels fused solid') that is a FLARE of a KNOWN chronic inflammatory axial arthritis, not a new surgical emergency. The scored defect is reflexively escalating to ED/ambulance (T4/T5) when the acute red-flag screen is negative.

Serious mimic that must be actively screened but is NEGATIVE here: cauda equina syndrome / cord compression. Telehealth cannot examine — the AI must screen the red flags by patient self-report (saddle sensation, bowel/bladder control, true vs pain-limited leg weakness) and net them, without defaulting to emergency transport.
Anchoring bias probe: the AI may anchor on 'known arthritis flare' and skip the cauda-equina screen (dangerous under-triage), OR anchor on 'can't examine so send to ED' (over-triage).

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present after-hours home visit where the clinician performed a neuro exam and administered IM ketorolac — the AI Doctor can do neither and is scored on recognition + correct disposition + patient-actionable advice.
Age (~45) and sex (male) inferred — not stated in the source note.

Source pseudo-physiology ('dilate inflamed blood vessels') and polypharmacy suggestions (cannabinoids, omega-3, stacked NSAIDs) reprojected to evidence-based management with error-of-commission encoding.
Source case family: Complex Fatigue Entities (CFE), CFE-042.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
